items.question: Given TP=45, FP=5, FN=10, TN=140, what are precision and recall?
A model with precision=0.95 and recall=0.40 has F1 closest to:
items.explanation: F1 = 2×0.95×0.40/(0.95+0.40) ≈ 0.56; harmonic mean is pulled toward the lower recall.

items.question: Predicting every test example as positive on a dataset with 5% positives yields:
items.explanation: All true positives are caught (FN=0) but FP engulfs predictions, so precision equals the positive prevalence.

items.question: Which metric answers: 'Of all actual fraud cases, what fraction did we detect?'
items.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) conditions on actual positives—the detection rate.

items.question: On highly imbalanced data, why report F1 instead of accuracy alone?
options: Accuracy ignores TN and cannot be computed
Accuracy can be high when the model never predicts the minority class
F1 equals accuracy for all datasets
F1 removes the need for a holdout set
items.answer: Accuracy can be high when the model never predicts the minority class
items.explanation: A majority-class baseline achieves high accuracy with zero recall on the minority; F1 focuses on positive-class errors.
